q: What is autoinflation therapy for the ear?
a: A technique for treating serous otitis media (glue ear) in which a patient inflates a nasal balloon to equalize middle ear pressure via the Eustachian tube
d: A procedure to introduce air into the middle ear via a needle through the eardrum
A surgical technique to enlarge the Eustachian tube opening to treat chronic dysfunction
A technique of gently blowing air into the external ear canal to remove a blockage
A method of inflating a balloon catheter inside a narrowed Eustachian tube
A pressure equalization procedure performed under general anesthesia in children
A technique of using positive airway pressure to ventilate the sinuses during sleep
e: Autoinflation is a non-invasive technique for treating otitis media with effusion (glue ear), particularly in children. The patient holds a special nasal balloon (e.g., Otovent) against one nostril and inflates it using nasal exhalation, which generates pressure that opens the Eustachian tube and allows fluid drainage from the middle ear. Clinical trials support its use as an adjunct to watchful waiting in children aged 3-11.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific clinical definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible Eustachian tube/middle ear alternatives.

q: What is vibroejaculation (penile vibratory stimulation)?
a: A semen collection technique in which a vibrating device applied to the glans penis triggers reflex ejaculation in men with spinal cord injuries
d: A surgical technique for harvesting sperm directly from the epididymis under anesthesia
An electroejaculation procedure using rectal electrical stimulation to produce ejaculation
A technique for collecting semen by manual massage of the seminal vesicles
A pharmacological method of triggering ejaculation using intrathecal neostigmine
A procedure to unblock the ejaculatory ducts by transurethral resection
A microsurgical technique for repairing damaged vas deferens to restore ejaculation
e: Penile vibratory stimulation (vibroejaculation) is a non-invasive method for semen retrieval in men with spinal cord injuries at or above T10. A vibrating device is applied to the frenulum/glans of the penis at a specific frequency and amplitude to trigger a reflex ejaculation arc through the sacral spinal cord segments. It is the first-line method for sperm retrieval in eligible spinal cord injury patients, preferred over electroejaculation for its non-invasive nature.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific clinical definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible male reproductive medicine alternatives.

q: What is a pelvic binder used for?
a: A circumferential compression device applied around the pelvis to stabilize pelvic ring fractures and reduce life-threatening hemorrhage in trauma patients
d: A supportive garment worn to relieve chronic low back pain in pregnant women
A postoperative compression device used after hip replacement surgery
A device used to immobilize the sacroiliac joints in ankylosing spondylitis
A compression bandage applied to the thigh to control femoral hemorrhage
A rigid splint used to stabilize open pelvic fractures after surgical fixation
A device used to apply traction to both legs in bilateral femur fractures
e: A pelvic binder (or pelvic circumferential compression device) is an emergency orthopedic device applied around the greater trochanters and pelvis to reduce pelvic volume in unstable pelvic ring fractures. This mechanical compression reduces the venous bleeding space, decreases blood loss, and provides some stability during transport. It is a key intervention in ATLS (Advanced Trauma Life Support) protocols for hemorrhagic shock from pelvic fractures.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific trauma medicine definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible pelvic/orthopedic device alternatives.

q: What does COVID-19 testing detect?
a: Infection with SARS-CoV-2, the coronavirus causing COVID-19, using PCR tests for viral RNA or antigen tests for viral proteins
d: Immunity to SARS-CoV-2 by measuring neutralizing antibody titers in blood
Previous exposure to SARS-CoV-2 using viral sequencing of recovered patients
The severity of active COVID-19 infection by measuring inflammatory biomarkers
Genetic susceptibility to severe COVID-19 by testing ACE2 receptor polymorphisms
Lung damage from COVID-19 using CT pulmonary angiography
The infective dose of SARS-CoV-2 needed to establish infection in an individual
e: COVID-19 testing diagnoses infection with SARS-CoV-2. Molecular tests (RT-PCR) detect viral RNA from nasopharyngeal swabs and are the gold standard for diagnosis. Rapid antigen tests detect viral proteins and give results in 15-30 minutes but are less sensitive. Serological antibody tests detect past infection or vaccine response. Testing was pivotal for pandemic surveillance, contact tracing, and infection control.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific diagnostic definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question to ask what it detects. Improved distractors to be plausible diagnostic testing alternatives.

q: What is pacing in chronic fatigue and pain management?
a: An activity management technique of balancing activity and rest to prevent boom-bust cycles and manage energy levels in patients with chronic fatigue or pain
d: A cardiac device-based procedure to regulate abnormal heart rhythms
A behavioral therapy technique using biofeedback to control pain responses
A graded exercise therapy program that progressively increases activity levels
A psychological relaxation technique for managing acute anxiety and pain
A physiotherapy technique using rhythmic movement to improve gait in neurological conditions
A pharmacological strategy of gradually increasing pain medication doses
e: Pacing (energy pacing or activity pacing) is a self-management technique used in chronic conditions such as fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS), and chronic pain. It involves identifying an individual's energy envelope and distributing activity within sustainable limits, alternating with planned rest — avoiding overexertion (which causes symptom flares) and underactivity. It is distinct from graded exercise therapy, which progressively pushes activity thresholds.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific clinical definition. Note: 'pacing' is ambiguous (also means cardiac pacing) — clarified as activity management technique. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors.

q: What is cerebral embolic protection?
a: A device-based technique using filters or deflectors deployed in cerebral arteries to capture emboli and prevent strokes during cardiac or vascular procedures
d: A pharmacological approach using anticoagulants to prevent stroke during atrial fibrillation
A surgical technique for clipping cerebral aneurysms before they rupture
A transcatheter technique for closing a patent foramen ovale to prevent paradoxical emboli
A clot retrieval procedure for treating acute ischemic stroke (mechanical thrombectomy)
A carotid endarterectomy technique using a proximal balloon to prevent emboli during surgery
A medical procedure using thrombolytic drugs to dissolve cerebral emboli after stroke
e: Cerebral embolic protection (CEP) refers to the use of mechanical devices during interventional cardiovascular procedures to capture or deflect embolic debris (thrombus, calcium, plaque particles) before it can reach the cerebral circulation and cause stroke. Examples include filter-based protection during transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR), carotid artery stenting, and cardiac surgery. CEP reduces peri-procedural stroke risk.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific clinical definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible cerebrovascular/stroke prevention alternatives.

q: What is the Ling 6-sound test used for?
a: A behavioral hearing assessment that evaluates a child's auditory detection ability across the speech frequency range using six representative phonemes
d: A standardized language test measuring vocabulary comprehension in children aged 2-5
An electrophysiological test measuring brainstem responses to auditory click stimuli
A speech discrimination test using monosyllabic words to measure word recognition
A standardized articulation test assessing production of phonemes in children
A behavioral assessment of selective auditory attention in preschool children
A hearing screening tool using pure-tone audiometry in frequency-specific bands
e: The Ling 6-sound test uses six phonemes (/ah/, /ee/, /oo/, /sh/, /ss/, /mm/) that span the frequency range of speech (250-8,000 Hz). It assesses whether a child can detect sounds across this range, which is especially important for monitoring hearing aid or cochlear implant function. A child who responds to all six sounds has adequate access to the full speech spectrum. It is a quick, functional bedside check for auditory detection.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific audiology definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible pediatric hearing assessment alternatives.

q: What is ovarian culture in assisted reproduction?
a: A laboratory technique in which ovarian follicles or immature oocytes are cultured in vitro to mature them for fertilization or research purposes
d: A procedure for testing ovarian tissue biopsies for cancer markers in the laboratory
A method of preserving ovarian tissue by freeze-drying for long-term storage
A test for measuring ovarian reserve by culturing granulosa cells from follicular fluid
A technique for banking live bacteria recovered from ovarian tissue cultures
A laboratory method for producing sex hormones from ovarian cells in cell culture
A method for isolating ovarian stem cells for regenerative medicine applications
e: Ovarian culture (in vitro maturation — IVM) involves retrieving immature oocytes from small antral follicles or ovarian tissue fragments and maturing them in specialized culture media outside the body. It is used as a fertility preservation strategy, particularly when ovarian stimulation is not possible (e.g., in patients at risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome). It avoids the need for gonadotropin stimulation.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific reproductive medicine definition. Original had empty statement description. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible reproductive medicine laboratory alternatives.

q: What is balloon-occluded retrograde transvenous obliteration (BRTO)?
a: An interventional radiology procedure that treats gastric varices by occluding the outflow vein with a balloon and injecting a sclerosant to thrombose the varices
d: A transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) procedure to lower portal pressure
An endoscopic banding technique to ligate gastric varices from inside the stomach
A surgical portocaval shunt procedure to decompress the portal system
An angiographic embolization of hepatic arterial blood supply to reduce portal hypertension
An endoscopic injection of cyanoacrylate glue into gastric variceal channels
A balloon tamponade technique using a Sengstaken-Blakemore tube to control acute variceal bleeding
e: Balloon-occluded retrograde transvenous obliteration (BRTO) is an image-guided procedure for treating gastric varices that have a gastrorenal or splenorenal shunt. A balloon catheter is advanced via the femoral vein into the shunt vessel, inflated to occlude outflow, and sclerosant (ethanolamine oleate or sodium tetradecyl sulfate) is injected to thrombose the varices. It is an alternative to TIPS for gastric variceal bleeding in appropriate patients.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific interventional radiology definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible gastric variceal treatment alternatives.

q: What is encephaloduroarteriosynangiosis (EDAS)?
a: A neurosurgical indirect revascularization procedure for Moyamoya disease in which a scalp artery is sutured to the dura to promote collateral blood vessel growth into the brain
d: A direct surgical anastomosis of the superficial temporal artery to the middle cerebral artery for ischemic stroke
A neurosurgical procedure to remove a brain arteriovenous malformation causing seizures
A carotid endarterectomy technique used to revascularize the brain after carotid stenosis
A procedure to bypass an occluded internal carotid artery using a saphenous vein graft
A surgical technique to clip a cerebral aneurysm in the circle of Willis
A procedure to implant a vascular stent across a stenotic intracranial artery
e: Encephaloduroarteriosynangiosis (EDAS) is an indirect revascularization surgical technique primarily used to treat Moyamoya disease, a rare progressive cerebrovascular condition in which the internal carotid arteries gradually occlude. A branch of the superficial temporal artery is dissected free while keeping it attached at both ends, laid on the brain surface, and sutured to the dura. Over months, new collateral vessels grow from the artery into the ischemic brain tissue.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific neurosurgical definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible cerebral revascularization alternatives.

q: What is total elbow arthroplasty?
a: A surgical procedure in which the damaged elbow joint is replaced with an artificial (prosthetic) implant to relieve pain and restore function
d: A surgical fusion (arthrodesis) of the elbow joint to eliminate painful motion
A procedure to repair the distal biceps tendon at its attachment to the radial tuberosity
A surgical procedure to repair a comminuted distal humerus fracture with plates and screws
A procedure to remove loose bone fragments from the elbow joint (loose body removal)
A procedure to release the lateral epicondyle to treat chronic tennis elbow (lateral epicondylitis)
A procedure to reconstruct the ulnar collateral ligament of the elbow in throwing athletes
e: Total elbow arthroplasty (TEA) is a joint replacement procedure in which the articulating surfaces of the humerus and ulna are replaced with linked or unlinked prosthetic components. It is primarily used for severe rheumatoid arthritis, distal humerus fractures in elderly patients, and post-traumatic arthritis when the elbow is too damaged for repair. It provides reliable pain relief but has higher complication and revision rates than hip or knee arthroplasty.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific orthopedic surgical definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible elbow procedure alternatives.

q: What is whole-body MRI used for?
a: A single imaging session using magnetic resonance imaging to scan the entire body, used for cancer staging, multiple myeloma assessment, or hereditary cancer screening
d: A full-body CT scan using ionizing radiation to screen for occult malignancies
A nuclear medicine PET scan using FDG to detect metabolic activity throughout the body
A standard MRI scan limited to the brain and spine in a single examination
A screening protocol using multiple ultrasound examinations of different body regions
A full-body scintigraphy using a radioisotope bone scan to detect bone metastases
A fluoroscopic examination of the entire GI tract from esophagus to rectum
e: Whole-body MRI (WB-MRI) is a non-ionizing imaging technique that acquires images from the head to the feet in a single examination session using multiple overlapping fields of view. It is the preferred method for staging multiple myeloma and related plasma cell disorders (replacing skeletal surveys), for detecting disseminated cancer, and for surveillance of hereditary cancer syndromes. Its lack of radiation makes it particularly valuable for repeated imaging.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific radiological definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question to ask what it's used for. Improved distractors to be plausible full-body imaging alternatives.

q: What is a cardiac pacemaker and how is it implanted?
a: An electronic device implanted under the skin near the collarbone that delivers electrical impulses to regulate abnormally slow or irregular heart rhythms
d: An external defibrillator device attached to the chest to terminate ventricular fibrillation
An implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) designed to treat life-threatening tachyarrhythmias
A temporary pacing wire inserted through a central vein to treat acute heart block
A leadless pacemaker capsule delivered via catheter directly into the right ventricle without chest surgery
A cardiac resynchronization device that paces both ventricles to treat heart failure
An implantable loop recorder that monitors heart rhythm without providing pacing therapy
e: A cardiac pacemaker is a small electronic device implanted subcutaneously (usually below the left clavicle) during a minor surgical procedure. Electrode leads are threaded through a central vein into the right atrium and/or right ventricle, where they sense intrinsic electrical activity and deliver timed electrical pulses when the heart rate falls below the programmed minimum. Indications include sick sinus syndrome, complete heart block, and symptomatic bradycardia.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific cardiological definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to distinguish pacemaker from other implantable cardiac devices.

q: What is the Ewing amputation?
a: A hindfoot amputation in which the calcaneus (heel bone) is preserved to allow weight-bearing on the heel stump, providing a functional residual limb
d: A transfemoral (above-knee) amputation at mid-thigh level for peripheral vascular disease
A Syme's ankle disarticulation amputation that removes the foot at the ankle joint
A Chopart amputation preserving the talus and calcaneus while removing the forefoot
A transtibial (below-knee) amputation for diabetic foot osteomyelitis
A Boyd amputation in which the calcaneus is fused to the tibia for a longer stump
A ray amputation removing one toe and its associated metatarsal bone
e: The Ewing amputation is a specialized orthopedic procedure for the foot and hindfoot in which the calcaneus (heel bone) is partially preserved to create a weight-bearing residual limb. By retaining the heel pad tissue and calcaneal bone, the patient can bear weight directly on the stump, potentially allowing mobility with minimal prosthesis. It is used for complex foot trauma, congenital foot deformities, and tumors of the midfoot.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific orthopedic surgical definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible foot/limb amputation alternatives.

q: What is oxygen insufflation in medicine?
a: The introduction of oxygen gas into a body cavity or tissue to treat a condition or facilitate a procedure, such as inflating the peritoneal cavity during laparoscopy
d: The delivery of oxygen through a face mask or nasal cannula for respiratory support
The hyperbaric oxygen therapy delivered in a pressurized chamber for wound healing
The instillation of ozone gas into a body cavity for antimicrobial therapy
The continuous positive airway pressure therapy for sleep apnea using compressed air
The administration of heliox (helium-oxygen mixture) to reduce airway resistance
The mechanical ventilation of a patient's lungs using a positive pressure ventilator
e: Oxygen insufflation refers to the delivery of oxygen into a body cavity or space. In laparoscopic surgery, carbon dioxide (not oxygen, due to fire risk) is the standard gas, but oxygen insufflation has been used in specific contexts such as peritoneal oxygenation for bowel ischemia, and transtympanic oxygen therapy for inner ear conditions. The term may also refer to supplemental oxygen delivery via insufflation catheters.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific clinical definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible oxygen delivery alternatives.

q: What is umbilical hernia repair?
a: A surgical procedure to close the abdominal wall defect at the navel through which abdominal tissue or bowel protrudes, using sutures or a mesh patch
d: A procedure to drain a fluid-filled cyst (urachal cyst) at the umbilicus
A surgical procedure to correct an umbilical granuloma in a newborn
A procedure to repair a paraumbilical hernia through an incision beside the navel
A laparoscopic procedure to repair an incisional hernia at a prior surgical scar
A procedure to close an inguinal hernia defect in the groin
A procedure to repair a Meckel's diverticulum discovered during abdominal surgery
e: Umbilical hernia repair is a surgical procedure to close a defect in the umbilical ring — the hole in the abdominal wall through which the umbilical cord passed. In infants, most umbilical hernias close spontaneously by age 5. In adults, repair is recommended for symptomatic, large, or complicated hernias. Primary suture repair (Mayo repair) or mesh reinforcement is used depending on defect size and patient factors.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific surgical definition. Original had empty statement description. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible abdominal wall hernia alternatives.

q: What is Davydov's colpopoiesis?
a: A neovaginoplasty technique developed by Russian surgeon S.N. Davydov that uses the peritoneum to surgically construct a neovagina in women with vaginal agenesis
d: A hysterectomy technique developed for minimally invasive removal of the uterus via the vaginal route
A vaginal reconstruction technique using a split-thickness skin graft (McIndoe procedure)
A surgical repair of vaginal prolapse by reattaching the vaginal vault to the sacral ligaments
A procedure to repair obstetric perineal tears using peritoneal flaps
A technique for dilating the vagina using progressive mechanical dilators (Frank's method)
A laparoscopic procedure to remove endometriotic implants from the vaginal fornices
e: Davydov's colpopoiesis (peritoneal neovaginoplasty) is a laparoscopic surgical technique for creating a functional neovagina in women with Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser (MRKH) syndrome or other causes of vaginal agenesis. The peritoneum of the rectouterine or vesicouterine pouch is mobilized laparoscopically and used to line the surgically created vaginal tunnel. It was developed by Soviet surgeon S.N. Davydov and is favored for good functional outcomes.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific gynecological surgical definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible vaginal surgery alternatives.

q: What is tenoplasty?
a: Surgical repair or reconstruction of a damaged or diseased tendon to restore its continuity, strength, and function
d: Surgical removal of a damaged tendon and replacement with a prosthetic tendon
Surgical transfer of a healthy tendon to compensate for a paralyzed muscle
A procedure to lengthen a shortened tendon using Z-plasty incisions
A procedure to release a contracted tendon sheath in trigger finger
An endoscopic procedure to debride tendon degeneration (tendinosis) without cutting the tendon
A procedure to reattach a completely torn tendon at its bone insertion
e: Tenoplasty (tendon plasty) is a surgical procedure that repairs, reconstructs, or reshapes a tendon that is torn, scarred, or too short. It may involve direct suture repair of a lacerated tendon, reconstruction using a tendon graft, or excision of scarred tendon with replacement by a silicone rod followed by staged tendon grafting. It is commonly performed for flexor tendon injuries of the hand.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific orthopedic/surgical definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible tendon surgery alternatives.

q: What is 3D mammography (digital breast tomosynthesis)?
a: A breast imaging technique that takes multiple low-dose X-ray images from different angles and reconstructs them into a 3D dataset, improving cancer detection and reducing false positives
d: A conventional 2D mammography exam using two views of each breast
A breast ultrasound technique using 3D volume data acquisition to characterize masses
A contrast-enhanced MRI technique used to screen high-risk women for breast cancer
A nuclear medicine scan using technetium sestamibi to detect multifocal breast cancer
A CT-guided stereotactic biopsy technique for sampling microcalcifications in 3D
A procedure using automated whole-breast ultrasound to screen dense breast tissue
e: Digital breast tomosynthesis (3D mammography) acquires multiple low-dose X-ray images of the breast from different angles (typically 15-25 degrees) and uses computer reconstruction to create a 3D image. Compared to 2D mammography, it improves cancer detection rates (especially in dense breast tissue), reduces recall rates due to overlapping tissue, and better characterizes lesion margins. It is now widely used as the standard of care for breast cancer screening.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific radiological definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible breast imaging alternatives.

q: What is anthelmintic treatment (deworming)?
a: The administration of antiparasitic drugs that kill or expel helminths (parasitic worms) from an infected host
d: The treatment of protozoal infections such as malaria with antiprotozoal drugs
The surgical removal of a tapeworm cyst (cyst) from the liver or brain
The administration of antibiotics to treat bacterial gastroenteritis
The use of antifungal agents to treat intestinal candidiasis
The prophylactic use of ivermectin to prevent mosquito-transmitted filarial disease
The decontamination of food and water sources to prevent helminth transmission
e: Anthelmintic (deworming) treatment uses pharmacological agents to kill or expel parasitic worms (helminths) including nematodes (roundworms), cestodes (tapeworms), and trematodes (flukes). Common anthelmintics include albendazole, mebendazole (for intestinal nematodes), praziquantel (for tapeworms and flukes), and ivermectin (for onchocerciasis, strongyloidiasis). Mass drug administration (MDA) programs use deworming to control neglected tropical diseases in endemic regions.
i: Original entry had Wikidata Q-number as name but description ('action of giving a substance intended to kill helminths') revealed the actual content. Rewrote as proper anthelmintic/deworming question. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Improved distractors to be plausible antiparasitic treatment alternatives.

q: What is involved in the diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder (ASD)?
a: A comprehensive behavioral and developmental assessment by specialists using standardized tools and clinical observation, as there is no single biological test for ASD
d: A single genetic test identifying mutations in known autism risk genes like SHANK3
A neuroimaging procedure using fMRI to identify specific brain activity patterns diagnostic of ASD
A blood test measuring biomarkers like serotonin or oxytocin levels to confirm ASD
A standardized cognitive test administered by a psychologist to measure IQ and adaptive function
An EEG examination identifying a specific brainwave signature pathognomonic for ASD
A brief pediatric screening questionnaire alone (M-CHAT) sufficient to diagnose ASD
e: Diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder (ASD) requires comprehensive multidisciplinary evaluation including developmental history, structured behavioral observation using tools like ADOS-2 (Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule) and ADI-R, and cognitive/adaptive function testing. There is currently no blood test, brain scan, or genetic test that alone can diagnose ASD. DSM-5 criteria require persistent deficits in social communication and restricted/repetitive behaviors present from early development.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific clinical definition emphasizing multi-modal assessment. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to challenge misconceptions about ASD diagnosis.

q: What is M. Ijaiya's technique in obstetrics?
a: A surgical modification for managing postpartum hemorrhage, used primarily in resource-limited settings where standard hemostatic procedures may not be available
d: A technique for repairing a fourth-degree obstetric perineal laceration
A shoulder dystocia maneuver for delivering an impacted fetal shoulder
A surgical technique for performing emergency cesarean hysterectomy
A vaginal breech delivery technique for managing trapped fetal head
A technique for manual removal of a retained placenta without general anesthesia
A suturing technique for closing a uterine scar from a prior cesarean section
e: M. Ijaiya's technique is a surgical procedure developed in West Africa for managing obstetric complications, particularly in resource-limited settings. The specific procedure is named after its developer and is used in cases where standard interventions for maternal hemorrhage or obstetric emergencies may be unavailable. It represents an example of locally adapted obstetric surgical innovation for low-resource healthcare settings.

q: What does the Auditory Steady-State Response (ASSR) measure?
a: The brain's electrophysiological response to amplitude-modulated tones, used to objectively estimate hearing thresholds at specific frequencies in infants and uncooperative patients
d: The brainstem's response to brief click stimuli used to detect retrocochlear pathology (ABR)
The cortical auditory response to speech sounds used to assess central auditory processing
The cochlear outer hair cell function using distortion product otoacoustic emissions
The middle ear pressure and mobility using tympanometry
The stapedius reflex threshold to assess acoustic reflex arc integrity
The auditory nerve compound action potential recorded from the cochlea (EcochG)
e: The Auditory Steady-State Response (ASSR) is an objective electrophysiological hearing test that measures the brain's steady-state response to rapid, amplitude- and/or frequency-modulated tones. Unlike the Auditory Brainstem Response (ABR), ASSR can be performed at multiple frequencies simultaneously and provides frequency-specific hearing threshold estimates. It is particularly valuable for fitting hearing aids in infants and young children who cannot respond behaviorally.
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific audiology definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question to ask what it measures. Improved distractors to be plausible audiological test alternatives.

q: What was pubiotomy historically used for?
a: A now-abandoned obstetric procedure in which the pubic bone was surgically cut to temporarily widen the pelvic outlet when a baby could not be delivered vaginally
d: A historical procedure to remove bladder stones through an incision above the pubis (suprapubic cystostomy)
A surgical amputation of the lower limbs through the pubic bones used in cancer surgery
A historical surgical procedure for repairing a symphysis pubis diastasis after pelvic fracture
A historical castration procedure performed for medical or social purposes
A historical surgical approach to the urinary bladder for removing bladder tumors
A procedure for resecting the pubic ramus in hemipelvectomy for sarcoma
e: Pubiotomy (symphysiotomy variant) was a historical obstetric operation in which a special instrument was used to cut through the pubic symphysis cartilage to increase the transverse diameter of the pelvis, allowing vaginal delivery when cephalopelvic disproportion prevented it. It was used in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, primarily in developing countries, as an alternative to cesarean section. It is now considered obsolete due to maternal morbidity (urinary incontinence, gait disturbance).
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific historical obstetric definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible pelvic/obstetric surgical alternatives.

q: What is an oesophagotomy?
a: A surgical incision into the esophagus, performed to remove a foreign body or open the esophageal lumen for direct access
d: Surgical removal of all or part of the esophagus (esophagectomy)
Surgical reconstruction of the esophagus using a stomach or bowel segment
Endoscopic incision of a Zenker's diverticulum to create a common channel (cricopharyngeal myotomy)
Endoscopic balloon dilation of an esophageal stricture
Surgical repair of a ruptured esophagus (Boerhaave syndrome)
Transection of the esophagus to control esophageal variceal bleeding (Sugiura procedure)
e: Oesophagotomy is a surgical procedure in which an incision is made through the wall of the esophagus to gain access to its interior. It may be performed to remove impacted foreign bodies or food boluses that cannot be removed endoscopically, to excise lesions of the esophageal wall, or as part of reconstructive surgery. It is distinct from esophagectomy (removal of the esophagus) or esophagoscopy (visual inspection).
i: Rewrote vague 'A medical procedure' answer with specific surgical definition. Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science. Rewrote question. Improved distractors to be plausible esophageal surgical alternatives.
